From sample 1:
We have the tremendous privilege of representing Nia Meltzer in connection with her sexual harassment claims against Local Union 28 in violation of the New York City Human Rights Law (“NYCHRL”) and New York State Human Rights Law (“NYSHRL”).

This letter outlines a series of concerning incidents of sexual harassment at Local Union 28 that transpired through

From sample 2:
We have the tremendous privilege of representing Dr. Duane Moise, a distinguished gastroenterologist practicing in North Carolina, and his wife, Mrs. Arline Moise. Dr. and Mrs. Moise are African American.
In 1866, in the aftermath of the Civil War, Congress passed the Civil Rights Act, 42 U.S.C. § 1981 (the “Act”). In securing the equal right to “make and enforce contracts,” the Act ensured that private economic relationships, including those between customers and car dealerships serving the public, would not be impacted by race discrimination.     
Nearly 100 years later, in 1963, President John F. Kennedy opined: “It ought to be possible for American consumers of any color to receive equal service in places of public accommodation, such as hotels and restaurants and theaters and retail stores.” Unfortunately, 157 years after the passage of the Act – which provides for a private right of action and uncapped punitive damages – and 65 years after President Kennedy spoke these compelling words, race discrimination persists at the Jaguar Hartford Dealership in Hartford, Connecticut (“JLR,” “Jaguar Hartford” or the “Dealership”). Under such circumstances, the need for vigorous enforcement of civil rights laws remains vital.
This letter is written to address a series of unsettling events evincing racial bias that transpired during and subsequent to my clients’ purchase of a 2023 Rivian R1S at Jaguar Hartford. The conduct at issue violates state and federal antidiscrimination laws – including, but not limited to, the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA), Connecticut General Statutes §§ 46a-58(a), 64 and 66.

II.  Inconsistencies with Findings 







The inconsistencies in the finding of Ms. Meltzer’s case raise significant concern regarding the transparency and the potential for Mr. Barba to return to work at  With Ms. Meltzer’s compelling presentation of screenshots and explicit messages during the Trial Committee proceedings,  held Mr. Barba guilty on 
three counts rendering him a fine of $30,000- and 26-hours training for sexual harassment.


There is an apparent discrepancy between the initial termination and final disciplinary measures, showing a lack of clarity in the Union’s handling of the situation. Local Union 28 has left open the possibility for Mr. Barba to return to his position. The absence of a definitive stance on his employment status leaves uncertainty which leads to the continued uneasiness Ms. Meltzer feels while at the Local Union. 

III.   Lack of Termination shows room for Negligence Retention
 
On August 28, 2021, Ms. Meltzer bega to experience harassment from Mr. Barba after exchanging numbers with him for work related purposes. Their interactions were strictly confined to professional contexts, as they had never met outside of a work-related event. Even when Ms. Meltzer changed her phone number, Mr. Barba managed to obtain it due to necessary communication about her Covid status following a work event they had both attended. She explained the unwanted messages, calls, and explicit messages she was receiving to her coworkers.
Despite knowing the extent of the harassment endured by Ms. Meltzer over approximately eight months, Local Union 28 left the possibility open for Mr. Barba’s return. This leniency raises concerns about the union’s commitment to protect its members from harassment. Given Mr. Barba’s prolonged and aggressive behavior, there is a fear that if he is reinstated, he will continue to engage in similar offenses. 





On April 13, 2022, Ms. Meltzer met with the Jersey City Police Department because of the immense amount of harassment she was placed under. By seeking assistance from the police department, shows the impact Mr. Barba’s action had on her well-being.

From sample 2:
Timeline of Discriminatory Conduct
Thursday, January 25th: Dr. Moise initiated contact with Jaguar Hartford expressing intent to purchase a 2023 Rivian R1S and informed the sales representative that his wife, with a power of attorney, would complete the sale on his behalf.
Friday, January 26th: Upon arrival at your dealership Mrs. Arline Moise faced challenges with the acceptance of a legitimate power of attorney and was required to tender a $1,000 deposit. In an act of racial animus, Dealership staff suggested that the Moises’ lawful power of attorney was fraudulent and refused to honor it. Notwithstanding, your general manager, Mr. Aaron Gettinger, eventually agreed that a remote sale would be organized, yet no follow-up communication occurred to initiate this process.
Monday, January 29th: Dr. Moise notified your salesperson, Kim, of the Dealership’s failure to contact him and made arrangements to complete the sale in person on January 30th.
Tuesday, January 30th: Despite providing ample evidence of his financial wherewithal and resources, Dr. Moise discovered his credit had been run without consent due to his race. Notwithstanding, a sale was eventually consummated. Exercising his First Amendment rights, Dr. Moise later left a fair and truthful review of the Dealership on Google noting the racial animus exhibited by its staff.
Wednesday, January 31st: General Manager Aaron Gettinger contacted my client about his truthful online review. Mr. Gettinger attempted to negotiate the removal of specific commentary regarding racial profiling, subsequently threatening to withhold the vehicle in retaliation if the demands were not met. Later, a telling voicemail to Mrs. Moise from another manager at Jaguar Hartford, Kyle, revealed that the Dealership did not intend to honor its agreement to convey the vehicle to the Moises. 
Thursday, February 1st: Mr. Gettinger repeatedly and disruptively telephoned Dr. Moise. Dr. Moise reiterated his preference for written communication and confirmed his intention to pick up the vehicle in person.
Friday, February 2nd: A series of e-mails from Mr. Gettinger conveyed conflicting messages regarding the release of the vehicle and continued the pattern of racism and retaliation. Once Gettinger finally assured the Moises that they could pick up their vehicle, Mr. Gettinger refused to release it pending payment from the bank. It was obvious that these shenanigans were traceable to racism 
Later that day, after Connecticut State Senator Marilyn Moore contacted the Dealership at Dr. Moise’s request, the Dealership reversed its stance and agreed to release the vehicle to my clients. The Dealership could not justify its conduct to Senator Moore.

IV. Local Union 28’s Conduct Violates the Law


 This incident illustrates Local Union 28’s continuing disregard for protecting its employees from sexual harassment perpetuated by another one of its employeesThe lack of protection from Mr. Barba's actions violates the New York State Human Rights Law (“NYSHRL”), and the New York City Human Rights Law (“NYCHRL”).

From sample 1:
IV.          Conclusion 
Our client is genuinely devastated by Local Union 28’s lack of regard and protective measures. Ms. Meltzer committed to achieving complete vindication for the contemptible actions to which she was subjected. To that end, we are prepared to commence litigation immediately, and we are certain that a jury would render a large award in our clients’ favor, including punitive damages - which are uncapped under the NYSHRL and NYCHRL.
We have nonetheless sent this letter in a good faith effort to resolve this matter without litigation.

From sample 2:
Conclusion
Our clients are genuinely devastated by the Dealership’s mistreatment of them, which obviously resulted solely from the color of their skin. They are committed to achieving complete vindication for the egregious violation to which they have been subjected.  To that end, we are prepared to commence litigation immediately, and we are certain that a jury would render a large award in their favor.
We have nonetheless sent this letter to present JLR with an opportunity to avoid costly and protracted litigation that is likely to result in substantial liability.
